items.rationale: He built a framework to determine when validators can be safely reused across services, exposing an 'overcollateralization factor' as a tunable robustness metric — sees it as a tractable, formalizable design problem.
items.age_note: Most recent sustained theme (Aug–Sep 2024); freshest in corpus.

citations.quote: New paper with @n_durvasula offers a framework to reason about the risks of restaking. Key question: Under what conditions can validators be safely reused across multiple services?
My work on restaking risks with @n_durvasula now has an accompanying talk, from the SBC Consensus workshop

items.rationale: His central, multi-year research obsession — building a 'hierarchy of permissionlessness' and proving what consensus guarantees (finality, accountability, partition-resilience) are achievable under which participation assumptions; views PoS PBFT-style protocols in the quasi-permissionless setting as the rich frontier.

citations.quote: Can PoS PBFT-style protocols do better? Tl;dr: yes, on all counts, at least if you're willing to adopt the stronger assumptions of the quasi-permissionless (QP) setting!
We propose a four-level hierarchy (Perm < QP < DA < FP) that parameterizes the amount of knowledge that a protocol has about who's running it

items.rationale: Genuinely thrilled at theory-to-practice translation — hard theory (Jolt/lookups) becoming 'the fastest zkVM in the world.' Treats it as a standout success of the field.

citations.quote: It's been amazing watching 102 pages of hard theory get turned into the fastest zkVM in the world.
when he calmly told me, 'I think this could be big,' I knew to take him very, very seriously. And now... the lookup singularity is here.

items.rationale: Long-running enthusiasm; he closed his original open question (no TFM can be DSIC+MMIC+OCA-proof) and extended TFM theory to a post-MEV world with active block producers and searchers — sees it as a deep, still-productive design space.

citations.quote: Major update to the paper... on transaction fee mechanism design in a post-MEV world (i.e., with active block producers), now with searchers... and tight welfare guarantees
the main open theory question from my original work on EIP-1559 has been resolved... no transaction fee mechanism can be DSIC, MMIC, and OCA-proof!

items.rationale: Fascinated by why Dutch auctions dominate web3 and by designing auctions robust to seller manipulation (shill bids) through mechanism design rather than appeals to 'rule of law.'

citations.quote: Why are Dutch auctions so popular in Web3?... A new paper with Andrew Komo and @skominers answers all these questions
To what extent can these deviations instead be disincentivized through an auction's design?

items.rationale: Actively developing a theoretical foundation for PBS, a core piece of the MEV-management stack.
items.age_note: Stated once (Jan 2024), part of ongoing TFM/MEV agenda.

citations.quote: Toward a theory of proposer-builder separation (with @bahrani_maryam and @PGarimidi)

items.rationale: He proved that with MEV, no non-trivial transaction fee mechanism can be incentive-compatible for both users and block producers — making good UX/bidding fundamentally hard and forcing reliance on encrypted mempools, OFAs, TEEs, MPC.
items.age_note: Core result July 2023, reaffirmed Feb 2024 as a standing caveat.

citations.quote: with MEV, *no* non-trivial or approximately welfare-maximizing TFM can be both DSIC and BPIC!
do we truly need any of these? Yes! And... we have the math to prove it

items.rationale: Same framework that excites him also surfaces the danger: a small shock (e.g., a slashing bug) can cascade across shared validators into catastrophic loss unless overcollateralization buffers are maintained.

citations.quote: When is a restaking network 'robustly secure,' meaning that a small "shock"... cannot enable a catastrophic attack?
the maximum-possible length of a cascade of attacks is also governed by the overcollateralization factor

items.rationale: Proved slashing cannot achieve targeted punishment (EAAC) in the dynamically-available or partially-synchronous settings — once an adversary is large enough to violate consistency, it can evade punishment; implies withdrawal delays must scale with worst-case message delay.

citations.quote: once an adversary is big enough to cause a consistency violation, it is big enough to evade targeted punishment
slashing in a proof-of-stake protocol cannot achieve its intended purpose if message delays cannot be bounded a priori

items.rationale: Showed in-protocol defense requires exotic, non-'time-malleable' cryptography (VDFs as in Chia, ephemeral keys as in Algorand); otherwise one falls back on out-of-protocol social consensus / checkpointing.

citations.quote: fancy primitives like VDFs and ephemeral keys are the *only possible way* for proof-of-stake blockchains to defend against long-range attacks without any out-of-protocol assistance

items.rationale: Any protocol that works under fluctuating participation (longest-chain, PoW or PoS) provably cannot offer finality/accountability, optimistic responsiveness, or partition-resilience — and Bitcoin's guarantees are only ever probabilistic.

citations.quote: if your blockchain protocol is robust to fluctuating participation... then it cannot be optimistically responsive, accountable, or robust to network partitions
Bitcoin's probabilistic guarantees are an unavoidable consequence of its total lack of assumptions about who might be running it

items.rationale: Not a price view — a design endorsement. He frames Ethereum as aspiring to a 'best of both worlds' consensus design and his slashing work makes precise the belief that the Merge increased Ethereum's economic security.

citations.quote: Ethereum is effectively aspiring toward the best of both worlds: the basic guarantees of a longest-chain protocol... plus the extra benefits of a PBFT-style protocol
making precise the common belief that the merge has increased Ethereum's economic security

risk_regime.rationale: Roughgarden is a theory/mechanism-design researcher; across 18 months he expresses zero market-directional posture — no buy/sell, bull/bear, or risk appetite statements. His 'positions' are technical (which protocol designs are sound, which mechanisms are provably impossible). Inferring a risk-on/off stance would be unsupported extrapolation.

notes: This is the view of an academic researcher, not a trader or investor — every 'excited'/'concerned' entry is about the soundness and tractability of a design problem, not asset returns. Recency weighting: 2024 work (restaking risks Aug–Sep, slashing/EAAC May, shill-proof auctions Apr, post-MEV TFMs Feb) is the freshest and most heavily weighted; the permissionless-consensus and PoS hierarchy program runs continuously Apr-2023→2024 and is his dominant structural theme. No genuine stance reversals were found — the body of work is cumulative and consistent (e.g., the MEV impossibility result is restated as a standing caveat in 2024 rather than retracted). Tokens are barely actionable: he names Bitcoin, Ethereum, Algorand (ephemeral keys), Chia (VDFs), Avalanche only as technical exemplars of design choices, not as investments; only Ethereum carries a mild positive design judgment, included with low conviction. tokens_concerned left empty because his critiques (e.g., Bitcoin's probabilistic-only guarantees) are protocol-design observations, not market warnings.
